gm.name: John Spytek
gm.title: General Manager
gm.previous_role: Assistant General Manager, Tampa Bay Buccaneers

career_path.mentor: Andy Reid, Tom Heckert Jr., John Harbaugh, Steve Spagnuolo, Sean McDermott
John Elway (GM), Matt Russell (Dir. Player Personnel)
John Elway (GM), Matt Russell, Gary Kubiak (HC)

scouting_tree.primary_mentor: Jason Licht (Tampa Bay Buccaneers GM, 2016-2024) — spent 9 seasons as Licht's top lieutenant, overseeing scouting departments and contributing to all draft and free agency decisions
scouting_tree.secondary_mentors: Tom Heckert Jr. (Philadelphia/Cleveland), Andy Reid (Philadelphia coaching staff), John Elway/Matt Russell (Denver)
scouting_tree.philosophical_lineage: Eagles scouting tree (Andy Reid era) → Heckert tree → Elway/Broncos tree → Licht/Buccaneers tree. The Licht influence is the strongest: premium positions in Round 1, elite athletic testing (RAS scores 9.25+ for first-rounders since 2016), secondary and OL targeted in Round 2, willingness to reach for character/tape matches. Eagles DNA shows in emphasis on competitive culture and 'football guys.' Broncos DNA shows in exposure to the Shanahan/Kubiak zone-run system that now drives the Raiders' offense under Klint Kubiak.
scouting_tree.breakpoints: More aggressive trade-down tendencies than Licht showed — traded back twice in Round 2 in his first draft (37→48→58). Licht rarely traded down that aggressively. Also more willing to draft FCS/small-school players (2 FCS QBs in 2025 Day 3) than Licht typically was. Explicitly stated he won't penalize older prospects, whereas Licht leaned younger in Round 1.
scouting_tree.lineage_weight_notes: Only 1 draft as Raiders GM. Licht/Buccaneers tree heavily weighted. Will drop to 0.50 after 2026 draft and 0.30 after 2027 draft as own track record accumulates.

front_office.primary_decision_maker: John Spytek — GM with final say on draft picks

key_influencers.name: Klint Kubiak
key_influencers.title: Head Coach
key_influencers.known_biases: Offensive-minded, runs Shanahan-style West Coast offense with outside zone run scheme. Values athletic OL who can move laterally, dual-threat RBs who can catch, and receivers who block. Heavy user of 12 personnel (2 TE sets) and fullback. Just won Super Bowl LX as Seattle OC. First-year HC, so scheme demands will heavily drive offensive draft priorities.

key_influencers.name: Tom Brady
key_influencers.title: Minority Owner
key_influencers.known_biases: College teammate of Spytek at Michigan. Played key role in GM and HC searches. Spytek has deep personal relationship with Brady dating back 25+ years. Brady's influence is more on big-picture organizational direction than individual picks, but his presence in the building adds a layer of QB evaluation credibility.

key_influencers.name: Brian Stark
key_influencers.title: Assistant General Manager
key_influencers.known_biases: Former Denver Broncos Director of College Scouting (since 2016). Reunited with Spytek in Las Vegas. Brings Broncos scouting perspective and college evaluation expertise. Likely handles day-to-day scouting operations.

key_influencers.name: Anthony Patch
key_influencers.title: Senior Personnel Executive
key_influencers.known_biases: 23-year Eagles scouting veteran, former Senior Director of College Scouting for Philadelphia. Reunited with Spytek — both came up in Eagles scouting ranks under Andy Reid and Tom Heckert from 2005-09. Brings Eagles-style evaluation rigor and college scouting depth.

key_influencers.name: Rob Leonard
key_influencers.title: Defensive Coordinator
key_influencers.known_biases: Promoted from within (was defensive line and run game coordinator under Pete Carroll in 2025). Switching Raiders to a 3-4 base defense, which changes positional needs — particularly at LB and EDGE.

front_office.power_dynamics: Collaborative but GM-led. Spytek stated his job is to 'give Klint as many good players as we can that fit his vision and his scheme.' Kubiak will have significant input on offensive personnel given his scheme specificity. Brady involved in big-picture direction. Mark Davis is relatively hands-off on individual picks but was involved in hiring decisions.
front_office.owner_interference: low-to-medium — Mark Davis drove the GM and HC search processes and has Tom Brady as a minority owner who is culturally influential, but neither appears to override individual draft picks

draft_philosophy.stated_philosophy: Team-building approach — not pure BPA and not pure need. Cited Ron Wolf as an influence. 'We're not going to pass on elite talent because we maybe have a couple guys at that spot, but we're certainly not going to just force picks because we need a player.' Emphasized 'understanding the value of certain positions.'
draft_philosophy.actual_philosophy: In his first draft (2025), took Ashton Jeanty at 6 — the best RB in the class but also the Raiders' biggest offensive need (worst rushing offense in NFL). Then traded down twice in Round 2 to accumulate picks, prioritizing volume of Day 2 selections over picking higher. Addressed needs at WR, CB, OL, DT, and LB across the draft. The Round 1 pick was equal parts BPA and need — Jeanty was both. Round 2-3 picks addressed clear needs (WR, CB, OL). This suggests a need-conscious approach dressed in BPA language. Spytek said he would've 'felt bad' if he didn't draft linemen — explicitly need-driven statement.
draft_philosophy.philosophy_mismatch_notes: Claims to not draft for need but explicitly acknowledged needing linemen and WRs. His first draft addressed every major need (RB, WR, CB, OL, DT, QB, LB) in order of urgency. This is a need-aware team-builder who uses BPA language, not a true BPA drafter.

trade_triggers: Board falling favorably in the range below — traded back when value was clustered in later picks
Desire to accumulate Day 2 and Day 3 picks for roster-building during a rebuild
No must-have player at current slot — willingness to slide back if board allows
trade_behavior.draft_capital_philosophy: Strongly prefers accumulating picks over consolidating. In 2025, turned 2 Day 2 picks into 4 Day 2 picks plus additional Day 3 capital. Spytek said 'the more picks the better' and 'we turned two picks today into four.' This aligns with his stated rebuild timeline (2028 playoff window). Tampa Bay under Licht was also active in trading but more balanced — Spytek appears even more trade-down oriented, at least during a rebuild phase.

position_avoidance: RB in Round 1 was historically avoided under Licht (Ronald Jones in R2, Bucky Irving in R4 were the norm) but Spytek broke this pattern immediately by taking Jeanty at 6 — though context matters (Carroll's run-first mandate + generational talent + biggest need). This should not be projected forward as a default tendency.
position_premium: OL and DL are clearly premium — Spytek stated he'd feel bad without drafting linemen. Licht's Tampa Bay invested heavily in DT (Vea, Kancey), OT (Wirfs), and CB in early rounds. 5 of Licht's last 6 first-round picks were in the trenches. Spytek said 'understanding the value of certain positions' is key.

QB.archetype_summary: Experienced, poised pocket passer who can operate a play-action/bootleg-heavy system. Spytek's history is overwhelmingly free-agency QBs (Brady, Mayfield, Vick, Fitzpatrick) over drafted ones. When QBs were drafted in his orbit, they tended to be developmental (Trask R2 2021, two FCS QBs in R6 2025). For franchise-caliber QBs, Spytek has shown preference for proven commodities. The 2026 #1 pick (likely Mendoza) represents his first true franchise QB draft decision. Spytek stated he prefers not to 'run a young QB out there right away' and wants to 'limit the amount of pressure on that guy.'

physical_profile.height_range: 6-1 to 6-5 — inferred from Kubiak's QB history (Cousins 6-3, Darnold 6-3, Carr 6-3). Kubiak has worked almost exclusively with 6-2+ pocket passers.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No data — not a typical QB filter
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Arm strength is paramount in the Shanahan/Kubiak system for deep shots off play-action. Mobility to execute bootlegs matters more than 40 time.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No speed requirement — pocket mobility and escapability valued over raw speed

playing_style.primary_style: Play-action-centric pocket passer who can execute bootlegs and work the middle of the field
most_valued_traits: Accuracy in the 10-20 yard range
Play-action execution and sell
Pocket composure under pressure
Decision-making speed/processing
Toughness/willingness to take hits
least_important_traits: Designed rushing ability — Kubiak's system uses bootlegs but not designed QB runs
deal_breakers: Run-first QBs who can't operate from under center
QBs who struggle with play-action concepts and pre-snap reads
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Insufficient data — Spytek has never drafted a franchise QB

college_production.production_floor: Must have been a multi-year starter — Spytek at 2026 combine described ideal QB as 'a leader, tough as hell, somebody that loves to play football, maniacal preparer'
college_production.production_trajectory: Values consistency and winning — 'you want a winner' (Kubiak on Mendoza)
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — will weigh supporting cast and conference strength

examples: 2025 R6.213 — Tommy Mellott, QB/WR, Montana State: FCS dual-threat who may convert to WR. Developmental flier.
2025 R6.215 — Cam Miller, QB, North Dakota State: FCS QB, 3,251 yards, 33 TDs, 4 INTs. Developmental.
Tampa influence: Kyle Trask R2 2021 — 6-5, 239, pocket passer from Florida, developmental backup behind Brady.

QB.confidence_note: Spytek has never selected a franchise QB. His history is almost entirely acquiring QBs via free agency (Brady, Mayfield). The 2026 Mendoza pick will be his defining QB decision. Kubiak's scheme heavily informs the archetype — play-action, under-center system.

EDGE.archetype_summary: Versatile, high-motor pass rusher who can stand up in a 3-4 or put hand in dirt. The new 3-4 defense under Rob Leonard demands EDGE players who can rush and drop. The Buccaneers under Licht/Spytek valued explosive, athletic EDGE rushers — Joe Tryon-Shoyinka (R1 2021, 6-5, 259, 9.30 RAS), Chris Braswell (R2 2024, 6-3, 250, Alabama). Spytek's 2025 Raiders draft did not address EDGE early (Maxx Crosby still on roster), but Hemingway (DT/EDGE hybrid) showed preference for versatile defenders.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 32.5 inches — Licht era heavily valued length (JTS 34.25" arms, Braswell 33.5" arms)
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosive athleticism — high RAS scores. Vertical jump (34"+) and broad jump (120"+) as explosion indicators. 3-cone under 7.10 preferred.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.65 or faster preferred based on Licht-era EDGE picks

playing_style.primary_win_condition: Explosive first step and power at the point of attack, with versatility to stand up or rush with hand down in a 3-4
most_valued_traits: First-step explosion/get-off
Motor and relentlessness
Ability to convert speed to power
Versatility to stand up or put hand in dirt
Run defense capability
least_important_traits: Refined pass rush repertoire — Licht/Spytek drafted raw athletes (JTS, Braswell) and developed them
deal_breakers: One-dimensional rushers who can't set the edge in the run game
Stiff athletes with poor bend and change of direction
Character concerns at premium positions
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite explosion/first step — Licht consistently drafted high-RAS EDGE players above consensus

college_production.production_floor: 5+ sacks in final season for Round 1-2 picks based on Licht-era data
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production preferred — breakout junior/senior seasons valued
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — will adjust for scheme and usage

examples: Tampa R1 2021 — Joe Tryon-Shoyinka: 6-5, 259, 4.63 40, 34.25" arms, 9.30 RAS. Washington product with explosive first step.
Tampa R2 2024 — Chris Braswell: 6-3, 250, Alabama. Athletic pass rusher with power and bend.
Tampa R3 2019 — Anthony Nelson: 6-7, 271, Iowa. Long, powerful, high-motor.
No EDGE drafted by Spytek in 2025 (Crosby still on roster).

EDGE.confidence_note: Primarily Licht-era data. The 3-4 scheme change under Leonard may shift archetype toward stand-up OLBs. Spytek's own EDGE preferences yet to be established.

OT.archetype_summary: Elite athlete who can move laterally in a zone-run scheme. The Licht/Spytek era produced Tristan Wirfs (R1 2020, 6-5, 320, 9.74 RAS) — one of the best OTs in the NFL. Kubiak's outside zone system demands OTs who can reach-block and move in space. Spytek drafted Charles Grant (FCS OT, 6-5) in R3 and Caleb Rogers (OL, Texas Tech) in R3 of 2025, signaling willingness to take developmental OL projects and small-school linemen who fit zone-blocking traits.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 33.5 inches — Wirfs had 33.75" arms. Licht strongly valued arm length at OT.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Lateral agility is paramount for zone blocking. Short shuttle and 3-cone critical. Explosiveness (vertical 30"+, broad jump 108"+).
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff but athletic profile must be exceptional — Wirfs ran 4.85 at 320 lbs

playing_style.primary_style: Athletic, zone-scheme blocker who can reach defenders and move laterally with quickness
most_valued_traits: Lateral mobility and foot speed
Zone-blocking technique and reach ability
Athleticism to pull and get to the second level
Anchor strength to hold point in pass protection
Versatility to play multiple OL positions
least_important_traits: Pure power/mauling style — zone scheme values movement over anchoring
deal_breakers: Stiff, non-athletic linemen who can't execute zone blocks
Short arms that limit reach at the point of attack
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing at the OT position — Wirfs-level RAS scores make Spytek/Licht pull the trigger

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter preferred. Rogers started 55 consecutive games at Texas Tech.
college_production.production_trajectory: Values durability and consistency over raw talent with limited starts
college_production.context_sensitivity: Willing to take FCS tackles (Charles Grant from FCS) if athletic profile fits zone scheme

examples: Tampa R1 2020 — Tristan Wirfs: 6-5, 320, 4.85 40, 33.75" arms, 9.74 RAS. Iowa. All-Pro RT.
2025 R3.99 — Charles Grant, OT, FCS: 6-5. Spytek said Grant is 'a perfect fit for the Raiders' scheme.'
2025 R3.98 — Caleb Rogers, OL, Texas Tech: 55 consecutive starts. Expected to play guard.
Tampa R2 2015 — Donovan Smith: 6-6, 338, OT. Long-tenured starter.

OT.confidence_note: Wirfs is the gold standard from Licht era. Kubiak's zone scheme makes lateral mobility the primary filter. Grant and Rogers from 2025 suggest willingness to develop Day 2-3 linemen.

IOL.archetype_summary: Athletic interior linemen who can pull and execute zone-blocking concepts. The Kubiak system demands guards and centers who can move laterally. Licht's Tampa Bay drafted Ali Marpet (R2 2015, 9.92 RAS, Hobart — Division III), Cody Mauch (R2 2023), and Luke Goedeke (R2 2022). Spytek himself drafted Caleb Rogers (OL, Texas Tech) in R3 2025, projected to play guard. The pattern: athletic linemen from non-traditional programs who fit zone-blocking traits.

physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 32 inches — less strict than OT requirements
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Short shuttle and 3-cone for lateral agility. Broad jump for explosiveness. Ali Marpet (9.92 RAS) set the Licht-era standard for IOL athleticism.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No hard 40 cutoff — values quickness and agility over straight-line speed

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-scheme interior lineman who can pull, reach-block, and get to the second level
most_valued_traits: Lateral mobility to execute zone blocks
Ability to pull and lead on outside zone runs
Anchor strength for pass protection
Football IQ and communication ability (especially at center)
Toughness and nastiness in the run game
least_important_traits: Pure phone-booth power — zone scheme doesn't require holding ground as primary skill
deal_breakers: Heavy-footed linemen who can't move laterally
Players with poor balance who get washed out of zone blocks
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing — Marpet from Division III Hobart was a massive reach by draft position but his 9.92 RAS justified it

college_production.production_floor: Multi-year starter. Rogers had 55 consecutive starts.
college_production.production_trajectory: Durability valued. Willing to take small-school linemen (Marpet from Hobart, Rogers from Texas Tech) if athletic profile is elite.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — willing to draft FCS/non-Power 5 IOL if testing supports projection

examples: 2025 R3.98 — Caleb Rogers, IOL, Texas Tech: 55 consecutive starts, projected guard in zone scheme.
Tampa R2 2015 — Ali Marpet, G, Hobart: 6-4, 307, 9.92 RAS. Division III. All-Pro guard.
Tampa R2 2022 — Luke Goedeke, G, Central Michigan: Mauling run blocker who developed into starter.
Tampa R2 2023 — Cody Mauch, G, North Dakota State: FCS guard with zone-blocking athleticism.
Tampa R1 2024 — Graham Barton, OL, Duke: 6-5, 314. Versatile interior lineman.

IOL.confidence_note: Strong Licht-era pattern of athletic IOL from non-traditional programs. Kubiak's zone scheme reinforces this preference. Rogers from 2025 confirms pattern continuation.

DT.archetype_summary: Two types: (1) Massive, space-eating nose tackle who can anchor and command double teams (Vita Vea archetype — 6-4, 347, R1 2018) and (2) Undersized, explosive penetrating 3-technique who disrupts with quickness (Calijah Kancey archetype — 6-1, 280, R1 2023). Licht/Spytek's Tampa Bay drafted DTs in Round 1 twice — the only position doubled in R1. Spytek's 2025 draft took two DTs: Tonka Hemingway (R4, South Carolina, hybrid DT/FB) and JJ Pegues (R5, Ole Miss, versatile DT who also played FB). The new 3-4 defense needs a nose tackle.

physical_profile.weight_range: 280-350 — Kancey at 280 on one end, Vea at 347 on the other
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No consistent minimum — Kancey was undersized everywhere, Vea was massive
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: For penetrating 3-tech: 3-cone, short shuttle, first-step quickness. For nose: anchor strength, play strength metrics. RAS scores: Vea 9.53, Kancey 9.59 — both elite athletes at their size.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No 40 cutoff — interior quickness matters more than straight-line speed

playing_style.primary_style: Disruptive interior presence — either through gap penetration (3-tech) or space control (nose)
most_valued_traits: Interior pass rush ability (sacks/pressures)
First-step quickness off the snap
Ability to command double teams and free up linebackers
Motor and effort consistency
Versatility to play multiple interior alignments
least_important_traits: Coverage ability — not asked of DTs in any scheme
deal_breakers: One-gap-only players in a scheme that requires some two-gap ability
Low-motor players who take plays off
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite interior disruption — Licht took Kancey at 19 despite 6-1, 280 frame because his disruption rate was Aaron Donald-like

college_production.production_floor: Must show pass rush production — Kancey had 14.5 TFLs and 7.5 sacks as a junior. Hemingway had 9.5 sacks in last 3 years.
college_production.context_sensitivity: Will adjust for scheme/alignment — Hemingway converted from TE to DL

examples: Tampa R1 2018 — Vita Vea: 6-4, 347, 5.10 40, 9.53 RAS. Washington. Anchor nose tackle.
Tampa R1 2023 — Calijah Kancey: 6-1, 280, 9.59 RAS. Pittsburgh. Penetrating 3-technique.
Tampa R2 2022 — Logan Hall: 6-6, 283. Houston. Versatile DL.
2025 R4.135 — Tonka Hemingway, DT, South Carolina: Converted TE, 9.5 sacks in last 3 years, also used as short-yardage FB.
2025 R5.180 — JJ Pegues, DT, Ole Miss: Versatile, 7 rushing TDs as short-yardage FB. 314 lbs.

DT.confidence_note: Strong Licht-era DT drafting record (Vea and Kancey are stars). Spytek's own 2025 DT picks were Day 3 developmental types. 3-4 defense may shift preference toward larger nose tackles.

CB.archetype_summary: Long, fast, athletic cornerback with elite speed and length. Spytek's first CB pick (Darien Porter, R3 2025) is the defining data point: 6-3, 195, 33" arms, 4.30 40, former WR convert — raw but with elite physical tools. This mirrors the Licht-era approach of targeting athletic CBs: Vernon Hargreaves (R1 2016, 5-10, 204, 9.27 RAS), Carlton Davis (R2 2018, 6-1, 206), Jamel Dean (R3 2019, 6-1, 206, elite speed). Spytek clearly prioritizes length+speed over polish.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-11 to 6-3 — Porter at 6-3 suggests Spytek values extreme length. Licht-era average was 6-0+.
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: 31 inches — Porter had 33" (95th percentile). Licht-era CBs had above-average arm length.
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: 40-yard dash is the primary filter. Porter ran 4.30. Speed is non-negotiable. Broad jump (131" for Porter) and vertical (36.5") for explosiveness.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster — likely a hard cutoff. Porter at 4.30 suggests Spytek will prioritize elite speed.

playing_style.primary_coverage_style: Man coverage with length and physicality. Porter projects as a press-man outside CB. The old Pete Carroll scheme demanded long CBs; the new Rob Leonard defense will likely continue valuing length.
most_valued_traits: Elite speed/recovery speed
Length and physicality at the catch point
Ball skills (Porter was a former WR with natural ball-tracking)
Ability to match vertical routes
Willingness to tackle in run support
least_important_traits: Refined technique — willing to draft raw athletes and develop them (Porter had only 3 years at CB)
deal_breakers: Slow corners regardless of technique — speed is the baseline
Short corners without compensating elite athleticism
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite speed+length combination — Porter was drafted in R3 despite being a relatively raw CB convert, purely on physical tools

college_production.production_floor: No clear statistical floor — Porter had only 51 career tackles and 3 INTs. Film and athleticism weighted far above stats for CBs.
college_production.production_trajectory: Willing to take late converts and developmental prospects (Porter switched from WR to CB before 2022 season)
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — will weight quality of competition and coverage role

examples: 2025 R3.68 — Darien Porter, CB, Iowa State: 6-3, 195, 4.30 40, 33" arms. Former WR convert. 3 INTs in final season.
Tampa R1 2016 — Vernon Hargreaves III: 5-10, 204, 9.27 RAS, Florida. Press-man corner.
Tampa R2 2018 — Carlton Davis III: 6-1, 206, Auburn. Physical press corner.
Tampa R3 2019 — Jamel Dean: 6-1, 206, Auburn. Elite speed, 17 passes defensed as rookie.
Tampa R5 2022 — Zyon McCollum: 6-2, 199, Sam Houston State. Long, athletic, small-school corner.

CB.confidence_note: Porter pick is extremely telling — 6-3 with 4.30 speed and 33" arms is a clear physical archetype statement. Combined with Licht-era CB drafting pattern, strong confidence in long/fast/athletic CB preference.

S.archetype_summary: Physical, explosive safety who combines coverage ability with striking power. Spytek spoke glowingly about T.J. Ward at Cleveland — 'the way that he could strike ball carriers and blockers was just something that I've never seen anybody else do in a body as compact as his' (5-10, 200). Licht's best safety pick was Antoine Winfield Jr. (R2 2020, 5-9, 203) — undersized but explosive and instinctive. Pattern: explosive, physical safeties with great instincts, not prototypical size.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-9 to 6-2 — range reflects willingness to draft undersized safeties (Winfield 5-9, Ward 5-10)
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — instincts and explosion matter more than length at S
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Explosion metrics: vertical jump, broad jump. Winfield had a 36" vertical. Short shuttle for change of direction.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster preferred — needs range to play single-high or cover ground

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile, physical safety who can play in the box, cover in space, and deliver violent hits
most_valued_traits: Explosive striking ability and physicality
Instincts and play recognition
Versatility to play box and deep
Ball skills and playmaking ability
Tackling reliability and run support
least_important_traits: Prototypical size — will draft undersized safeties if explosion and instincts are elite
deal_breakers: Poor tacklers/soft players who avoid contact
Safeties who can only play one role (box-only or deep-only)
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite explosion and instincts in a compact frame — Winfield and Ward both fit this mold

college_production.production_floor: Ball production valued — Winfield had 7 career INTs at Minnesota despite missing time with injury
college_production.production_trajectory: Values playmaking ability over counting stats

examples: Tampa R2 2020 — Antoine Winfield Jr.: 5-9, 203, Minnesota. All-Pro safety, explosive hitter.
Cleveland R2 2010 — T.J. Ward: 5-10, 200, Oregon. Spytek's personal favorite evaluation — explosive striker.
Tampa R2 2017 — Justin Evans: 6-0, 199, Texas A&M. Rangy ball-hawk.
Tampa R3 2024 — Tykee Smith: 5-10, 198, Georgia. Physical, instinctive defender.

S.confidence_note: Winfield and Ward are strong data points from Spytek's direct involvement. No safety drafted in 2025 (Pola-Mao re-signed). Pattern favors explosive, physical safeties regardless of size.

LB.archetype_summary: Athletic, fast, high-character linebacker who can run sideline-to-sideline. The Licht/Spytek Tampa Bay produced Devin White (R1 2019, 6-0, 237, 4.42 40, 9.33 RAS) and Lavonte David (extended, valued highly). White was the prototypical athletic LB pick. The 3-4 defense under Leonard will need LBs who can cover and blitz. Spytek's 2025 LB pick was his final selection — Cody Lindenberg (R7, Minnesota), a run-defending LB.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Speed: 4.50 or faster for premium picks (White ran 4.42). 3-cone and short shuttle for change of direction. Vertical jump for explosiveness.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.55 or faster for early-round LBs — sideline-to-sideline speed is essential

playing_style.primary_style: Sideline-to-sideline athlete who can cover, blitz, and run-defend in a 3-4 scheme
most_valued_traits: Speed and range to cover sideline-to-sideline
Coverage ability against TEs and RBs
Blitz ability and pass rush upside
Instincts and play recognition
High motor and competitive toughness
least_important_traits: Pure size/mass — will draft lighter LBs if speed and coverage ability are elite
deal_breakers: Slow, plodding thumpers who can't cover in space
Poor blitzers in a 3-4 scheme that demands OLB pressure
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite speed at LB — White at 4.42 was a top-5 pick partly due to his rare speed at 237 lbs

college_production.production_floor: High tackle totals preferred — Lindenberg was a standout at Senior Bowl. White had 134 tackles as a junior.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production with explosive final season
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — will adjust for scheme and usage

examples: 2025 R7.222 — Cody Lindenberg, LB, Minnesota: Run defender, Senior Bowl standout. 71 tackles, 4 TFLs in 2022.
Tampa R1 2019 — Devin White: 6-0, 237, 4.42 40, 9.33 RAS. LSU. Pro Bowl LB.
Tampa R3 2017 — Kendell Beckwith: 6-2, 243, LSU. Instinctive run defender.

LB.confidence_note: White is the primary data point but was a Licht pick in a different scheme. The 3-4 transition changes LB needs significantly. Lindenberg in R7 is thin evidence. Expect LB to be addressed more aggressively in 2026.

WR.archetype_summary: Tough, physical, high-character receivers who compete at the catch point and block in the run game. Spytek's 2025 picks: Jack Bech (R2, 6-1, 214, TCU — big-bodied, contested catch specialist, former TE, Senior Bowl MVP) and Dont'e Thornton Jr. (R4, Tennessee — deep threat, led nation in yards per catch at 25.4). The Licht era produced Chris Godwin (R3 2017 — tough slot who thrived in intermediate game) and Mike Evans (R1 2014 — big-bodied X receiver). Spytek stated he doesn't believe in building around a clear-cut No. 1 WR, preferring a committee approach.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-11 to 6-5 — wide range reflecting different roles (Bech 6-1, Thornton deep threat, Evans 6-5, Godwin 6-1)
physical_profile.weight_range: 190-220 — leans toward physical builds (Bech at 214 is built like a TE)
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No strict minimum — catch radius and body control matter more
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: For contested catch types: size/weight combo, vertical jump, catch radius. For deep threats: 40-yard dash speed (Thornton ran 4.30). Route running valued over raw speed for intermediate targets.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: No universal cutoff — values different speed profiles for different roles

playing_style.primary_style: Physical, competitive receivers who win contested catches, block willingly, and are reliable in high-leverage situations
most_valued_traits: Contested catch ability and ball skills
Blocking willingness and physicality in run game (critical in Kubiak zone-run scheme)
Route savvy and spatial awareness
Toughness and willingness to work the middle of the field
Low drop rate — Bech had 1.6% drop rate
least_important_traits: Pure speed without production — won't draft a track athlete who can't run routes
deal_breakers: Receivers who refuse to block — non-starter in Kubiak's run-heavy system
Character concerns or diva behavior — Spytek emphasized 'quality humans' and team-first mentality
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Senior Bowl/all-star game standout performance — Bech was Senior Bowl MVP and Spytek pulled the trigger

college_production.production_floor: 1,000+ yard season for Day 2 picks preferred — Bech had 1,034 yards in 2024. Thornton had 661 yards (deep threat role).
college_production.production_trajectory: Breakout senior seasons accepted — Bech broke out in 2024 after modest prior production. Spytek values development arcs.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — Bech's production at TCU was in a spread attack; Thornton's 25.4 YPC shows role-specific evaluation

examples: 2025 R2.58 — Jack Bech, WR, TCU: 6-1, 214, 31" arms. 1,034 yards, 9 TDs in 2024. Senior Bowl MVP. Former TE, physical contested-catch specialist.
2025 R4.108 — Dont'e Thornton Jr., WR, Tennessee: Deep threat, 661 yards, 25.4 YPC (led nation). 4.30 40-yard dash.
Tampa R3 2017 — Chris Godwin, WR, Penn State: 6-1, 209. Tough slot, route savvy, All-Pro.
Tampa R3 2024 — Jalen McMillan, WR, Washington: 6-1, 192. 8 TDs in rookie year.
Tampa R1 2014 — Mike Evans, WR, Texas A&M: 6-5, 231. Contested-catch monster, 7.83 RAS.

WR.confidence_note: Bech and Thornton picks establish physical/tough and deep threat archetypes respectively. Godwin from Licht era reinforces intermediate toughness preference. Spytek explicitly said Raiders won't build around a No. 1 WR — committee approach.

TE.archetype_summary: Receiving-capable hybrid who can also block. Tampa Bay under Licht/Spytek drafted O.J. Howard (R1 2017, 6-6, 251, Alabama, 9.76 RAS — elite athlete) and Cade Otton (R4 2022, Washington — reliable receiver with blocking ability). Kubiak's system uses heavy 12 personnel (27.4% of snaps in Seattle) so TE is a premium position in this offense. Brock Bowers is already on roster as a star receiving TE, so the archetype for any drafted TE would likely be a blocking/hybrid complement.

physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Athleticism is paramount — Howard had 9.76 RAS. Vertical jump (38"+) and broad jump for explosion. 40 time under 4.70 for receiving threats.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.70 or faster for receiving TEs; blocking TEs can be slower

playing_style.primary_style: Versatile TE who can line up inline, flex out, and contribute as both blocker and receiver in a 12-personnel-heavy offense
most_valued_traits: Inline blocking ability for zone-run scheme
Receiving ability in the intermediate game (seam routes, play-action targets)
Athleticism to stretch the field and create mismatches
Football IQ to handle multiple alignments
Durability
least_important_traits: Pure receiving production — with Bowers already on roster, next TE will need to block
deal_breakers: TEs who can't block — Kubiak's 12 personnel requires both TEs to contribute in run game
Stiff athletes who can't flex into different roles
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Elite athletic testing at TE size — Howard's 9.76 RAS was irresistible

college_production.production_floor: No strict floor — Otton had 678 yards as a senior. Howard had 595 yards. Production varies based on team's passing volume.
college_production.context_sensitivity: High — will account for offensive scheme and target competition

examples: Tampa R1 2017 — O.J. Howard: 6-6, 251, 4.51 40, 9.76 RAS. Alabama. Elite athlete at TE.
Tampa R4 2022 — Cade Otton: 6-5, 247, Washington. 1,446 receiving yards through first 3 seasons.
No TE drafted by Spytek in 2025 (Brock Bowers already on roster as star TE).

TE.confidence_note: Bowers' presence means TE drafting is low priority. If addressed, likely looking for blocking-first complement to pair in 12 personnel. Howard and Otton represent the Licht-era range.

RB.archetype_summary: Dynamic, all-purpose back with receiving ability who can thrive in a zone-run scheme. Spytek's first-ever first-round pick was Ashton Jeanty (R1.6, 5-8.5, 211, Boise State) — a generational talent with elite contact balance, vision, and receiving ability. Kubiak runs a two-back system (Walker/Charbonnet in Seattle) and wants backs who can make zone-read cuts. Licht's Tampa Bay drafted Bucky Irving (R4 2024, 5-10, 195, Oregon — zone-scheme fit) and Rachaad White (R3 2022, 6-0, 214). Pattern: versatile backs with receiving upside, not pure power backs.

physical_profile.height_range: 5-8 to 6-0 — Jeanty at 5-8.5 is on the shorter end; Irving at 5-10 is average; White at 6-0 is taller
physical_profile.arm_length_minimum: No minimum — not a relevant RB filter
physical_profile.key_athletic_tests: Contact balance and vision are more important than combine testing. Jeanty skipped 40 at combine. Lateral agility (short shuttle) matters for zone-read cuts. Receiving ability (routes, hands) valued.
physical_profile.speed_requirement: 4.50 or faster preferred but not a hard cutoff — Jeanty's projected 4.42-4.46 is adequate; Irving ran 4.55

playing_style.primary_style: Zone-scheme running back with vision, contact balance, and receiving versatility who can be a three-down player
most_valued_traits: Vision and patience behind zone blocks
Contact balance and ability to break tackles
Receiving ability out of the backfield (routes, screens)
Lateral agility for zone-read cutbacks
Competitive toughness and motor
least_important_traits: Pure top-end speed — won't draft a track athlete without vision and balance
deal_breakers: One-dimensional backs who can't catch or pass protect
Backs who only produce behind elite offensive lines — need to create on their own
playing_style.traits_that_make_them_reach: Generational production and film — Jeanty's 2,601 rushing yards, 50 career TDs, and film that showed elite contact balance justified a top-6 pick at RB

college_production.production_floor: For early-round picks: elite statistical production expected. Jeanty had 4,769 career rushing yards, 50 TDs. Irving had 1,180 yards as a junior.
college_production.production_trajectory: Ascending production preferred — Jeanty's trajectory was 821→1,347→2,601 yards
college_production.context_sensitivity: Moderate — Jeanty's Mountain West context was a question but his film vs. Oregon (192 yards) answered it

examples: 2025 R1.6 — Ashton Jeanty, RB, Boise State: 5-8.5, 211. 4,769 career rush yards, 50 TDs. Heisman runner-up. Led all rookies in rushing yards.
Tampa R4 2024 — Bucky Irving, RB, Oregon: 5-10, 195. 1,122 rookie rushing yards. Zone-scheme fit.
Tampa R3 2022 — Rachaad White, RB, Arizona State: 6-0, 214. Receiving-capable all-purpose back.
Tampa R3 2020 — Ke'Shawn Vaughn, RB, Vanderbilt: 5-10, 214. Zone-scheme runner (bust).

RB.confidence_note: Jeanty pick was heavily influenced by Pete Carroll's run-first mandate and the Raiders' league-worst rushing attack. Kubiak's two-back zone system reinforces the need for versatile backs. Future RB drafting likely in mid-to-late rounds to complement Jeanty.

reach_tolerance.notes: Spytek explicitly stated: 'whether or not other people think that we drafted guys a little bit too high, I think the proof was in the pudding.' This suggests willingness to draft 'their guy' regardless of consensus. Licht-era Tampa Bay consistently drafted players above consensus (Hargreaves, Aguayo, Tryon-Shoyinka) when conviction was strong. Bech at 58 may have been a slight reach by some boards. The 2025 draft's two trade-downs suggest Spytek will trade back to get value rather than reach — but when he picks, he picks conviction over consensus.

scheme_fit.offensive_scheme: Shanahan/Kubiak West Coast offense with outside zone run scheme as the foundation. Heavy play-action, bootlegs, under-center snaps, 12 personnel (2 TE sets), fullback usage. Kubiak's forte is the outside zone, adopted from his father Gary Kubiak who learned it from Mike Shanahan. Run game is a 'non-negotiable.' Demands athletic OL who can move laterally, versatile RBs, and receivers who block.
scheme_fit.defensive_scheme: Switching to 3-4 base defense under new DC Rob Leonard (promoted from defensive line/run game coordinator). Previously ran 4-3 under Patrick Graham. This scheme change affects personnel needs — more stand-up OLBs, different LB profiles, and nose tackle requirement.
scheme_fit.scheme_change_notes: MAJOR SCHEME CHANGES IN 2026: (1) New HC Klint Kubiak replacing Pete Carroll — same zone-run offensive philosophy but different play designs. (2) New DC Rob Leonard installing 3-4 defense to replace 4-3. (3) New OC Andrew Janocko. (4) Rick Dennison as run game coordinator/senior offensive advisor — 30 years of NFL experience in zone-run systems. All of these changes happened in February 2026. Draft priorities will shift to accommodate 3-4 defensive personnel.

psychological_profile.risk_description: Moderate risk-taker. Willing to draft raw athletes with upside (Porter at CB, FCS tackles, dual-threat QBs on Day 3) but first-round pick was the consensus-safest player on the board (Jeanty). Licht-era Tampa Bay took Kancey at 6-1/280 in Round 1 — a clear upside bet. Spytek appears willing to take calculated risks, especially in later rounds, but prefers safety in Round 1. Matt Russell (mentor) praised Spytek for 'going against the grain' on late-round evaluations.
psychological_profile.recency_description: Moderate. After a 3-14 season and league-worst offense in 2025, the 2026 draft will likely be heavily influenced by the need to fix the passing game (QB, WR, OL) and defense (CB, LB, S). The coaching change to Kubiak (zone-run specialist) could mitigate some recency bias by imposing scheme-based priorities over reactive ones.
psychological_profile.sunk_cost_behavior: Insufficient data as GM. At Tampa Bay, the front office moved on from busts relatively quickly (Hargreaves released after 3 seasons, Aguayo cut after 1 year). Spytek appears willing to cut losses — fired Pete Carroll after one season.
psychological_profile.combine_description: Above-average combine emphasis. Porter (4.30 40, 95th percentile arms) was the most athletic CB in the class. Licht-era Tampa Bay had a clear RAS floor of 9.25 in Round 1 since 2016. Spytek stated he watches players 'against future NFL players when the stakes are higher' — suggesting film-first with athletic testing as confirmation. But Porter pick was clearly driven by elite measurables over refined technique.
psychological_profile.character_description: Very high character emphasis. Spytek stated character evaluation is 'uncompromising' — values humility, passion for football, competitive spirit, love of the game. Said a bad interview typically won't affect his board but unknown medical issues will. Emphasized that NIL money makes character evaluation easier. Licht-era lesson: early busts (Seferian-Jenkins, Aguayo) taught the front office to weight character more heavily. Spytek co-founded the National CMV Foundation — personal tragedy has deepened his emphasis on human quality.
psychological_profile.quarterback_philosophy: Prefers acquiring franchise QBs through free agency (Brady, Mayfield history) but will draft one when circumstance demands it. At 2026 combine: 'I'm not necessarily in favor of running him out there right away' — wants to develop young QBs behind a veteran. Wants to surround a young QB with 'a great offensive line, a run game, a great defense.' This is a build-the-infrastructure-first approach to QB development, not a throw-him-in-and-see approach.

college_production_preferences.production_vs_projection: Balanced but leans toward projection when athletic profile is exceptional. Spytek said 'we matched the quality tape up with the quality person' at Tampa Bay — values the intersection of production, film, and character. Porter was a projection bet (raw CB convert with elite tools). Bech was a production bet (1,034 yards, Senior Bowl MVP). Jeanty was both production and projection (generational stats + elite film). When forced to choose, Spytek appears to lean slightly toward athletic projection if character checks out.

production_thresholds_by_position.WR: 1,000+ yards in final season for Round 2 picks (Bech had 1,034). Flexibility for deep threats with role-specific production (Thornton 661 yards, 25.4 YPC).
production_thresholds_by_position.RB: Elite production required for premium picks — Jeanty had 2,601 yards, 29 TDs.
production_thresholds_by_position.EDGE: 5+ sacks in final season for Round 1-2 based on Licht-era data.
production_thresholds_by_position.CB: No clear statistical floor — Porter had modest stats but elite tools. Film and athleticism override counting stats for CBs.
production_thresholds_by_position.DT: Must show pass rush production — TFLs and sacks expected.

college_production_preferences.notes: Spytek explicitly stated age doesn't 'detract a ton' from his evaluation. He pushed back on analytics department concerns about older prospects. This is unusual — most modern GMs weight age heavily. Bech was 22 at draft, Porter was 24, Rogers had 55 consecutive starts. Spytek values experience and maturity over youth.

CB.notes: Porter at 4.30/33" set the bar extremely high

OT.notes: Wirfs 33.75". Zone scheme demands long arms for reach blocks

WR.notes: Drafted WRs across athletic spectrum — Bech is a possession type, Thornton is a burner. Values blocking ability for all WRs.

DT.notes: RAS 9.25+ for Round 1 DTs (Vea 9.53, Kancey 9.59). No strict size minimum — 280-347 lbs range.

S.notes: Explosion metrics matter more than size. Will draft 5-9 safeties if athleticism is elite.

measurable_thresholds.threshold_type: Soft preferences rather than hard cutoffs for most positions. Exception: speed at CB appears to be a near-hard cutoff (4.50 or faster). RAS 9.25+ in Round 1 appears to be a Licht-era guardrail that may carry over.

size_preferences_by_position.DT: Wide range accepted: 280 (Kancey) to 347 (Vea). Scheme and role determine ideal size.
size_preferences_by_position.LB: 6-0, 230-245 range for athletic LBs. White was 6-0, 237.
size_preferences_by_position.S: No strong size preference — drafts 5-9 to 6-1 safeties
size_preferences_by_position.WR: 6-1+ preferred for outside, no minimum for slot. Bech at 214 lbs is unusually heavy.

measurable_thresholds.athletic_testing_favorites: 40-yard dash for CBs and WRs. RAS (Relative Athletic Score) appears to be a key composite metric — Licht's Round 1 picks averaged 9.25+ RAS from 2016 onward. Broad jump and vertical jump as explosion proxies across all positions. 3-cone and short shuttle for EDGE, LB, and IOL.
measurable_thresholds.measurables_vs_film: Film-first with measurables as confirmation and elimination tools. Spytek described himself as a 'film and scouting junkie.' However, Porter pick shows measurables can override film concerns when tools are elite — Porter was a raw CB convert drafted primarily on physical projection. The formula appears to be: film establishes the baseline, measurables can elevate (elite testing) or eliminate (failing thresholds).

round_strategy.round_1_strategy: Premium positions and high-impact players. In 2025, took the consensus best RB in the class who was also the biggest need. Will take the consensus top QB (Mendoza) at #1 overall in 2026. Not afraid to take non-traditional premium positions (RB, TE) if the player is clearly the best available. Licht-era: 78% of Round 1 picks were premium positions (QB, OT, WR, EDGE, DT, CB).
round_strategy.round_2_3_strategy: Aggressive trade-down to accumulate picks in this range. In 2025, traded from 37→48→58 in Round 2, turning 2 picks into 4 on Day 2. Addresses needs but values volume of picks. This is where scheme-fit picks happen — Porter (CB), Bech (WR), Rogers (OL), Grant (OT) all addressed needs. Round 2-3 is the need-filling zone.
round_strategy.day_3_strategy: Developmental athletes, scheme-specific role players, and small-school/FCS prospects. In 2025: two DTs (Hemingway, Pegues — both versatile), two FCS QBs (Mellott, Miller — developmental), and a LB (Lindenberg — run defender). Spytek is known for 'finding late-round talent' per Matt Russell. Willing to take FCS players and position converts on Day 3.
round_strategy.compensatory_pick_usage: Used both 2025 comp picks (R6) on FCS QBs — developmental, higher-risk picks. Suggests willingness to use comp picks for project players.

RB.note: Jeanty led all rookies in rushing. Single-pick sample.

WR.note: Bech had 20 receptions for 224 yards as a rookie — struggled. Thornton showed deep threat potential.

CB.note: Porter is a developmental project, not expected to start immediately.

OL.note: Rogers and Grant are developmental. Rogers described as 'not missing a day' of training.

DT.note: Hemingway showed promise with sacks late in season. Pegues contributed.

blind_spots: WR evaluation is unproven — Bech's rookie season was poor (20 receptions). Need more data.
strengths: RB evaluation strong (Jeanty success + Licht-era Bucky Irving). DT evaluation strong via Licht lineage (Vea, Kancey). OL evaluation strong via Licht lineage (Wirfs, Marpet, Goedeke).

free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_spending_philosophy: Moderate-to-aggressive spender during rebuild. Has ~$90-100M in cap space for 2026. Traded for Geno Smith (3rd-round pick) as veteran QB in 2025. Kubiak wants Kenneth Walker III from Seattle in free agency to run two-back system. Spytek said 'you can pass in free agency thinking you're going to get one in the draft and then leave a massive hole on your roster.' This suggests he'll use FA aggressively to fill immediate needs, then draft for long-term building.
free_agency_draft_interaction.fa_draft_correlation: When a veteran starter is signed, the position drops in draft priority but doesn't disappear entirely. Expects to fill 1-2 major holes via FA (QB veteran mentor, RB complement) and then draft a mix of BPA and remaining needs.
free_agency_draft_interaction.roster_construction_philosophy: Build through the draft at premium positions, supplement through free agency. Two-year growth window with realistic playoff target of 2028. Spytek stated his job is to 'give Klint as many good players as we can that fit his vision and his scheme.' This is a draft-centric rebuild supplemented by targeted FA signings.

notable_patterns: Traded down twice in Round 2, turning 2 picks into 4 Day 2 selections — strong trade-down tendency
7 offensive players, 4 defensive players — heavily addressed offense in first draft
Drafted from every level: Power 5 (TCU, Iowa State, Texas Tech, Tennessee, Ole Miss, Minnesota), FCS (Montana State, North Dakota State, FCS OT)
Two DTs and two QBs drafted — positional doubling on Day 3
No EDGE, S, or TE drafted — positions of relative strength on roster (Crosby, Bowers)
Stated he would've 'felt bad' without drafting linemen — 4 of 11 picks were OL or DL

biggest_reaches.consensus_rank: Viewed as late Day 3/UDFA by some boards
biggest_reaches.context: 55 consecutive starts at Texas Tech, fits zone-blocking scheme. Some analysts called it a reach.

biggest_steals.consensus_rank: Early R2 value per some analysts (Big Blue View called him 'early second round value')
biggest_steals.context: 6-3, 4.30 40, 33" arms. Raw but elite physical tools. Some boards had him as R2 player.

confidence_notes: Only 1 draft as Raiders GM (2025). That draft was made with Pete Carroll as HC — a different scheme and philosophy than the current Kubiak regime. Scouting tree (Licht/Tampa Bay) provides the strongest signal for evaluation tendencies. Kubiak's scheme demands will heavily influence 2026 picks, particularly on offense. The 3-4 defensive switch changes positional needs. Profile should be refreshed substantially after the 2026 draft. Free agency moves before the draft will clarify remaining needs and reveal BPA vs. need tendencies more clearly.
